task: Stage-1 original coarse ASCR (4x4 -> dilation -> 32x32 token reopen) with the base GENERATOR swapped Show-o -> MMaDA-8B, keeping the Qwen3.5-9B VLM as the selector (everything else unchanged)
clean_pass_criterion: Single-image strict prompt-following judge returns {matches_prompt, score 0-1}; PASS when score >= 0.5 (objects, counts, colors, attributes, spatial relations, semantics).
architecture_note: MMaDA (transformers 4.46) and Qwen3.5-9B (transformers 5.2.dev fork) are incompatible in one process (MMaDA remote code lacks all_tied_weights_keys under 5.2.dev). So each pair uses TWO GPUs: a resident MMaDA worker (.venv-mmada) + a resident Qwen eval server (.venv-qwen36) communicating over a file IPC dir (ascr/evaluators/remote_eval.py + scripts/judge/qwen_eval_server.py). 4 nodes x 8 GPU = 16 pairs, models loaded once each.

generation.selector: Qwen3.5-9B VLM (max_new_tokens=768, repair 1024), coarse grid 4x4, dilation=1, max_selected_cells=8

conclusion: Swapping ONLY the generator (Show-o -> MMaDA-8B) while keeping the original Qwen-9B coarse ASCR selector gives a real, non-regressive gain: +6.2pp clean (33->37 / 51.6%->57.8%) and 2 wins / 0 losses / 62 ties vs baseline. This is markedly better than letting MMaDA judge itself (Phase 8 direct-self and Phase 9 coarse-self both stayed at 51.6% and 0/0/64 ties). I.e. the external Qwen-9B selector is what drives the ASCR improvement; the gain seen for Show-o ASCR transfers to MMaDA-8B with the selector unchanged.
caveats: The MMaDA baseline (51.6%) is selector-INDEPENDENT (baseline = initial generation, no revision); the selector only affects the revised arm. So the earlier choice of MMaDA-self vs Qwen does not change the baseline.

This baseline uses generation_timesteps=15 at 512px for speed; the historical Show-o numbers used 50 steps and a different judge/prompt rubric, so 51.6% here is NOT directly comparable to those numbers. A strict Show-o-vs-MMaDA comparison would require re-judging Show-o under this identical clean Gemini judge.
